purpose: Convert agent cognitive failure modes into explicit debt items that enterprises can accept, remediate, or defer as technical risk rather than vague intuition.

debt_classes.title: Hallucinated claims or artifacts
observability_signals: Generated statements cite assets that do not exist.
Runtime or governance output conflicts with repo truth.
mitigation: Require trace-linked evidence refs before release claims.
Promote unverifiable claims into explicit review findings.
Escalate to human approval when evidence replay fails.

debt_classes.title: Critical omission or partial reasoning
observability_signals: Required packets or receipts are absent from the chain.
Plans skip boundary assumptions or rollback steps.
Monitoring blind spots are discovered after deployment.
mitigation: Use structured plan drafts and required field contracts.
Record omissions as debt items until they are closed.

debt_classes.title: Over-inference or speculative action
observability_signals: The agent extrapolates beyond contract scope.
Execution is attempted before approval or ownership is clear.
Proposals imply capability that no service actually exposes.
mitigation: Use control-plane contracts to cap action scope.
Require explicit approval state before apply paths unlock.
Prefer recommendation registries over central runtime ownership.

debt_classes.title: Authority or boundary misread
observability_signals: The agent treats advisory context as source of truth.
Central governance attempts to own service-local runtime.
mitigation: Keep source-of-truth registries machine-readable and fixed.
Trace all approval and ownership receipts.
Reject central runtime ownership creep during review.

operating_rules: Every material agent failure pattern may be logged as cognitive debt only if it is observable and reviewable.
Debt items must describe mitigations and a clear enterprise decision state.
The ledger informs adoption and packaging decisions; it does not replace runtime incident handling.
